items.q: If a car has negative acceleration, what does that necessarily mean?
items.correct: Its acceleration is in the negative direction (often opposite to the chosen positive direction); it may be slowing down or speeding up in reverse.
items.misconception: Negative acceleration always means the car is slowing down.
items.random_wrong: Negative acceleration means the car is moving backwards.
items.why: Students conflate 'deceleration' with 'negative acceleration'; sign indicates direction relative to the chosen axis, not necessarily slowing.

items.q: If an object is moving at constant velocity in a straight line, what is the net force on it?
items.correct: Zero — by Newton's first law, constant velocity requires zero net force.
items.misconception: There must be a forward force on the object to keep it moving.
items.random_wrong: The net force equals the velocity times the mass of the object.
items.why: This is the classic 'impetus' misconception — everyday experience (objects slow down due to friction) leads students to believe motion requires a continuous force.

items.q: For a projectile launched horizontally, what affects the horizontal component of velocity during flight (ignoring air resistance)?
items.correct: Nothing — horizontal velocity stays constant; only vertical velocity changes due to gravity.
items.misconception: Gravity slows down both the vertical and horizontal motion.
items.random_wrong: The horizontal velocity increases over time because the projectile is falling.
items.why: Students treat 'gravity affects all motion' too broadly, missing that gravity acts only vertically and the components are independent.
It's Tiredness. “Wet road” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Acceleration due to gravity on Earth is approximately:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 9.8 m/s². “1 m/s²” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: A car at 10 m/s decelerates at 2 m/s² and stops. Using v² = u² + 2as, what distance does it travel before stopping, in metres?
items.why: The real figure is 25. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
